title: Esther 1
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Esther
paragraphs: 1 This happened in the days of Xerxes, who ruled 127 provinces from India to Cush.
2 In those days King Xerxes sat on his royal throne in the citadel of Susa.
3 In the third year of his reign, Xerxes held a feast for all his officials and servants. The military leaders of Persia and Media were there. The nobles and princes of the provinces also came.
4 For a full 180 days he showed off the great wealth and grand glory of his kingdom.
5 At the end of this time, the king held a seven-day feast for all the people in the citadel of Susa. The feast was in the garden court of the royal palace, from the least to the greatest.

6 Hangings of white and blue linen were fastened with cords of fine white and purple material to silver rings on marble pillars. Gold and silver couches were set on a mosaic pavement of porphyry, marble, mother-of-pearl, and other costly stones.
7 Drinks were served in gold goblets, each with a different design. The royal wine flowed freely, by the king's order.
8 By order of the king, no limit was placed on the drinking. Every official was to serve each man what he wanted.
9 Queen Vashti also gave a feast for the women in the royal palace of King Xerxes.
10 On the seventh day, when the king's heart was happy with wine, he ordered the seven royal servants who served him — Mehuman, Biztha, Harbona, Bigtha, Abagtha, Zethar, and Carkas —
11 to bring Queen Vashti before him, wearing her royal crown, to show her beauty to the people and officials. For she was beautiful.
12 But Queen Vashti refused to come at the king's command, brought by his royal servants. The king became furious, and his anger burned within him.
13 Then the king asked the wise men who knew the times. It was his custom to consult the experts in law and justice.
14 His closest advisors were Carshena, Shethar, Admatha, Tarshish, Meres, Marsena, and Memucan. These seven princes of Persia and Media had personal access to the king and ranked highest.
15 "According to law," he asked, "what should be done with Queen Vashti? She refused to obey the king's command brought by the royal servants."
16 In front of the king and his princes, Memucan answered, "Queen Vashti has done wrong not only to the king, but to all the princes and peoples in all the king's provinces.
17 For the conduct of the queen will become known to all women. They will look down on their husbands and say, 'King Xerxes ordered Queen Vashti to be brought before him, but she did not come.'
18 This very day the noble women of Persia and Media who have heard about the queen will say the same thing to all the king's officials. There will be much contempt and anger.

19 If it pleases the king, let him issue a royal order. Let it be written in the laws of Persia and Media so it cannot be changed. Vashti shall never again come before King Xerxes. Let her royal position be given to a woman better than she.
20 When the king's order is heard throughout his vast kingdom, all women, from the least to the greatest, will honor their husbands."
21 The king and his princes were pleased with this advice. So the king did as Memucan had said.
22 He sent letters to all the provinces of the kingdom. Each province got the letter in its own writing, and each people in their own language. Every man should be master of his own house.

title: Esther 2
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Esther
paragraphs: 1 Some time later, when King Xerxes' anger had cooled, he remembered Vashti, what she had done, and what had been ordered against her.
2 Then the king's attendants said, "Let a search be made for beautiful young women for the king.

3 Let the king appoint officers in each province of his kingdom. They will gather all the beautiful young women to the women's house at the citadel of Susa. Place them under the care of Hegai, the king's royal servant in charge of the women. Give them beauty treatments.
4 Then let the young woman who pleases the king become queen in place of Vashti." The king liked this idea, and he did so.
5 In the citadel of Susa there was a Jewish man from the tribe of Benjamin named Mordecai son of Jair, son of Shimei, son of Kish.
6 He had been carried into exile from Jerusalem by Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon. He was among those taken with Jeconiah king of Judah.

7 Mordecai had raised Hadassah (that is, Esther), the daughter of his uncle. She had no father or mother. The young woman was lovely in form and appearance. When her parents had died, Mordecai had taken her in as his own daughter.
8 When the king's command was announced, many young women came to the citadel of Susa, under Hegai's care. Esther was also taken to the palace and placed under Hegai, the keeper of the women.

9 The young woman pleased him and gained his favor. He quickly gave her beauty treatments and a special diet. He gave her seven chosen maids from the palace. He moved her with them to the best place in the women's house.
10 Esther did not tell anyone her nation or her family. Mordecai had told her not to.
11 Every day Mordecai walked back and forth in front of the women's house. He wanted to know how Esther was and what was happening to her.
12 Before her turn to go to King Xerxes, each young woman went through twelve months of treatments. Six months with oil of myrrh, then six months with perfumes and beauty products.
13 When the young woman would go to the king, she was given anything she asked to take from the women's house to the palace.

14 She would go in the evening. In the morning she returned to a second women's house under Shaashgaz, the king's royal servant in charge of the royal women. She would not return to the king unless he was pleased with her and called her by name.

15 Esther was the daughter of Abihail, the uncle of Mordecai who had adopted her. When it was her turn to go to the king, she asked only for what Hegai, the king's trusted officer, had advised. Esther found favor with everyone who saw her.
16 She was taken to King Xerxes in the royal palace in the tenth month, the month of Tebeth, in the seventh year of his reign.

17 The king loved Esther more than all the other women. She found grace and favor in his sight more than all the other young women. So he placed the royal crown on her head and made her queen in place of Vashti.
18 Then the king held a great feast, Esther's feast, for all his officials and servants. He gave a tax break in the provinces and handed out gifts from the king's wealth.
19 When the young women were gathered a second time, Mordecai was sitting at the king's gate.
20 Esther still had not told her family or her nation, just as Mordecai had told her. She obeyed Mordecai's command, as she had done under his care.
21 In those days, while Mordecai was sitting at the king's gate, Bigthan and Teresh, two of the king's royal servants who guarded the door, became angry and planned to kill King Xerxes.
22 When Mordecai learned of the plot, he told Queen Esther. She told the king on Mordecai's behalf.
23 When the report was investigated and proved true, both officials were hanged on a pole. All this was written in the Book of Records in the king's presence.

title: Esther 3
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Esther
paragraphs: 1 After these events, King Xerxes honored Haman son of Hammedatha, the Agagite. He raised him to a position above all the princes with him.
2 All the royal servants at the king's gate bowed down to Haman, because the king had commanded it. But Mordecai would not bow down.
3 Then the royal servants at the king's gate asked Mordecai, "Why do you disobey the king's command?"
4 Day after day they warned him, but he would not obey. So they told Haman to see if Mordecai's behavior would be allowed. He had told them he was a Jew.
5 When Haman saw that Mordecai would not bow down to him, he was full of anger.
6 When he learned the nation of Mordecai, he did not want to kill Mordecai alone. Instead, he planned to destroy all of Mordecai's people, the Jews, throughout the kingdom of Xerxes.

7 In the twelfth year of King Xerxes, in the first month, the month of Nisan, the Pur (that is, the lot) was cast before Haman. It was cast to find a day and a month. The lot fell on the twelfth month, the month of Adar.

8 Then Haman said to King Xerxes, "There is a certain people scattered among the peoples in every province of your kingdom. Their laws are different from everyone else's, and they do not obey the king's laws. It is not good for the king to leave them alone.
9 If it pleases the king, let an order be given to destroy them. I will pay ten thousand talents of silver into the royal treasury for those who do this work."
10 So the king removed the signet ring from his finger and gave it to Haman son of Hammedatha, the Agagite, the enemy of the Jews.
11 "Keep your money," said the king to Haman. "These people are given to you. Do with them as you please."

12 On the thirteenth day of the first month, the royal scribes were called. The order was written exactly as Haman commanded. It went to the king's provincial rulers, the governors of each province, and the officials of each people. It was written in the script of each province and the language of each people. It was written in the name of King Xerxes and sealed with the royal signet ring.

13 The letters were sent by messengers to each royal province. The order was to destroy, kill, and wipe out all the Jews — young and old, women and children — and to take their goods on one day, the thirteenth day of Adar, the twelfth month.
14 A copy of the order was to be issued in every province and shown to all the people, so they would be ready on that day.
15 The messengers left, hurrying by the king's command, and the order was issued in the citadel of Susa. Then the king and Haman sat down to drink, but the city of Susa was in confusion.

title: Esther 4
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Esther
paragraphs: 1 When Mordecai learned of all that had happened, he tore his clothes. He put on sackcloth and ashes and went out into the middle of the city, crying loudly and bitterly.
2 But he went only as far as the king's gate. The law did not allow anyone wearing sackcloth to enter that gate.
3 In every province where the king's order came, there was great mourning among the Jews. They fasted, wept, and grieved. Many lay in sackcloth and ashes.
4 When Esther's maids and royal servants came and told her about Mordecai, the queen was deeply troubled. She sent clothes for Mordecai to wear instead of his sackcloth, but he would not accept them.
5 Then Esther called Hathach, one of the king's royal servants assigned to her. She sent him to Mordecai to learn what was wrong and why.
6 So Hathach went out to Mordecai in the city square in front of the king's gate.
7 Mordecai told him all that had happened, including the exact amount of money Haman had promised to pay into the royal treasury to destroy the Jews.

8 Mordecai also gave Hathach a copy of the written order issued in Susa for the destruction of the Jews. He asked him to show and explain it to Esther. He urged her to go to the king, beg for his favor, and plead for her people.
9 So Hathach went back and told Esther what Mordecai had said.
10 Then Esther told Hathach to say to Mordecai,

11 "All the king's officials and the people of the king's provinces know one law. Any man or woman who goes to the king in the inner court without being called must be put to death. Only if the king holds out the gold scepter may that person live. And I have not been called to the king for the last thirty days."
12 When Esther's words were told to Mordecai,
13 he sent back this reply: "Do not think that because you are in the king's palace, you alone will escape the fate of all the Jews.

14 If you stay silent at this time, help and rescue for the Jews will rise from another place. But you and your father's family will perish. And who knows? Perhaps you have come to the kingdom for a time just like this."
15 Then Esther sent this reply to Mordecai:

16 "Go and gather all the Jews in Susa, and fast for me. Do not eat or drink for three days, night or day. I and my maids will fast in the same way. After that, I will go to the king, even though it is against the law. And if I die, I die!"
17 So Mordecai went and did everything Esther had told him.

title: Esther 5
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Esther
paragraphs: 1 On the third day, Esther put on her royal robes. She stood in the inner court of the palace, across from the king's quarters. The king was sitting on his royal throne in the royal courtroom, facing the entrance.

2 As soon as the king saw Queen Esther standing in the court, she found favor in his sight. The king held out the gold scepter in his hand toward Esther. She came forward and touched the tip of the scepter.
3 "What is it, Queen Esther?" the king asked. "What is your request? Even up to half the kingdom, it will be given to you."
4 "If it pleases the king," Esther replied, "may the king and Haman come today to the feast I have prepared for the king."
5 "Hurry," the king commanded, "and bring Haman, so we can do as Esther has asked." So the king and Haman went to the feast Esther had prepared.
6 As they drank their wine, the king said to Esther, "What is your request? It will be given to you. What do you ask for? Even up to half the kingdom, it will be done."
7 Esther replied, "This is my request:

8 If I have found favor with the king, and if it pleases the king to grant my request, may the king and Haman come tomorrow to the feast I will prepare for them. Then I will answer the king's question."
9 That day Haman went out happy and glad of heart. But at the king's gate he saw Mordecai, who did not rise or tremble in fear at his presence. Haman was full of anger toward Mordecai.
10 Yet Haman held back his anger and went home. He called for his friends and his wife Zeresh.
11 Haman told them about his great wealth, his many sons, and all the ways the king had honored and promoted him above the other officials and servants.
12 "What is more," Haman added, "Queen Esther invited no one but me to join the king at the feast she prepared. And I am invited back tomorrow with the king."
13 "Yet none of this satisfies me as long as I see Mordecai the Jew sitting at the king's gate."

14 His wife Zeresh and all his friends told him, "Have them build a hanging-pole 75 feet high. Ask the king in the morning to have Mordecai hanged on it. Then go to the feast with the king and enjoy yourself." The advice pleased Haman, and he had the pole built.

title: Esther 6
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Esther
paragraphs: 1 That night the king could not sleep. So he ordered the Book of Records, the Chronicles, to be brought in and read to him.
2 There it was found written that Mordecai had reported Bigthana and Teresh, two of the royal servants who guarded the king's entrance, when they had planned to kill King Xerxes.
3 The king asked, "What honor or reward has been given to Mordecai for this?" "Nothing has been done for him," his attendants replied.
4 "Who is in the court?" the king asked. Haman had just entered the outer court of the palace. He had come to ask the king to hang Mordecai on the pole he had prepared.
5 So the king's attendants answered, "Haman is there, standing in the court." "Bring him in," the king ordered.
6 Haman entered, and the king asked him, "What should be done for the man whom the king is glad to honor?" Haman thought to himself, "Whom would the king want to honor more than me?"
7 So Haman said to the king, "For the man whom the king is glad to honor,
8 have them bring a royal robe the king himself has worn, and a horse the king himself has ridden — one with a royal symbol on its head.

9 Let the robe and the horse be given to one of the king's most noble princes. Let them dress the man the king wants to honor and parade him on the horse through the city square. Let them shout before him, 'This is what is done for the man whom the king is glad to honor!'"
10 "Hurry," said the king to Haman, "and do just as you have said. Take the robe and the horse to Mordecai the Jew, who is sitting at the king's gate. Do not leave out anything you have suggested."
11 So Haman took the robe and the horse, dressed Mordecai, and paraded him through the city square. He shouted before him, "This is what is done for the man whom the king is glad to honor!"
12 Then Mordecai returned to the king's gate. But Haman rushed home, with his head covered in grief.

13 Haman told his wife Zeresh and all his friends everything that had happened. His advisers and his wife Zeresh said to him, "Since Mordecai is Jewish, you will not win against him. Your fall has already started. You will surely fall before him."
14 While they were still speaking, the king's royal servants arrived and rushed Haman to the feast Esther had prepared.

title: Esther 7
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Esther
paragraphs: 1 So the king and Haman went to dine with Esther the queen.
2 As they drank their wine on that second day, the king again asked, "Queen Esther, what is your request? It will be given to you. What do you ask for? Even up to half the kingdom, it will be done."
3 Queen Esther replied, "If I have found favor in your sight, O king, and if it pleases the king, grant me my life as my request, and the lives of my people as my request.
4 For my people and I have been given over to destruction, death, and wiping out. If we had only been sold as slaves, I would have stayed silent. Such trouble would not be worth bothering the king."
5 Then King Xerxes spoke up and asked Queen Esther, "Who is this? Where is the one who would plan such a thing?"
6 Esther replied, "The enemy is this wicked man — Haman!" And Haman stood in terror before the king and queen.

7 In great anger, the king got up from drinking his wine and went to the palace garden. Haman stayed behind to beg Queen Esther for his life. He saw that the king was planning a terrible fate for him.

8 Just as the king returned from the palace garden to the feast hall, Haman was falling on the couch where Esther was leaning. The king said, "Would he actually attack the queen while I am in the palace?" As soon as the words left the king's mouth, they covered Haman's face.

9 Then Harbonah, one of the royal servants attending the king, said: "There is a hanging-pole 75 feet high at Haman's house. He had it built for Mordecai, who gave the report that saved the king." "Hang him on it!" declared the king.
10 So they hanged Haman on the pole he had prepared for Mordecai. Then the king's anger went away.

title: Esther 8
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Esther
paragraphs: 1 That same day King Xerxes gave Queen Esther the property of Haman, the enemy of the Jews. Mordecai came into the king's presence, because Esther had told him about her family ties to Mordecai.
2 The king took off the signet ring he had taken back from Haman and gave it to Mordecai. Esther put Mordecai in charge of Haman's property.
3 Once again, Esther spoke to the king. She fell at his feet, weeping, and begged him to cancel the evil plan of Haman the Agagite against the Jews.
4 The king held out the gold scepter toward Esther. She rose and stood before the king.

5 "If it pleases the king," she said, "and if I have found favor with him, and if the matter seems right to the king, and I am pleasing to him, may an order be written to cancel the letters that Haman son of Hammedatha, the Agagite, wrote to destroy the Jews in all the king's provinces.
6 For how could I bear to see the disaster that would fall on my people? How could I bear to see the destruction of my family?"
7 So King Xerxes said to Queen Esther and Mordecai the Jew, "Look, I have given Haman's property to Esther. He was hanged on the pole because he attacked the Jews.

8 Now you may write in the king's name as you please about the Jews, and seal it with the royal signet ring. An order written in the name of the king and sealed with the royal signet ring cannot be canceled."

9 At once the royal scribes were called. On the twenty-third day of the third month (the month of Sivan), they wrote all of Mordecai's orders. The orders went to the Jews, the provincial rulers, governors, and princes of the 127 provinces from India to Cush. Each province got it in its own script, each people in their own language, and the Jews in their own script and language.
10 Mordecai wrote in the name of King Xerxes and sealed it with the royal signet ring. He sent the letters by messengers on swift horses bred from the royal mares.

11 By these letters the king allowed the Jews in every city to gather and defend themselves. They could destroy, kill, and wipe out any forces of any people or province who attacked them, including women and children, and take their goods.
12 The single day set throughout all the king's provinces was the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, the month of Adar.
13 A copy of the order was to be issued in every province and shown to all the people. The Jews were to be ready on that day to defend themselves against their enemies.
14 The messengers rode out in haste on their royal horses, pressed on by the king's command. The order was also issued in the citadel of Susa.
15 Mordecai went out from the king's presence in royal robes of blue and white, with a large gold crown and a purple robe of fine linen. The city of Susa shouted and rejoiced.
16 For the Jews it was a time of light and gladness, of joy and honor.

17 In every province and every city, wherever the king's order reached, there was joy and gladness among the Jews, with feasting and celebrating. And many of the people of the land joined the Jewish people, because the fear of the Jews had fallen on them.

title: Esther 9
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Esther
paragraphs: 1 On the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, the month of Adar, the king's order was to be carried out. On this day the enemies of the Jews had hoped to overpower them. But their plan was turned around, and the Jews overpowered those who hated them.
2 In each of the king's provinces, the Jews gathered in their cities to attack those who sought to harm them. No one could stand against them, because the fear of them had fallen on all peoples.
3 All the officials of the provinces, the provincial rulers, governors, and the king's officials helped the Jews. The fear of Mordecai had fallen on them.
4 Mordecai held great power in the palace. His fame spread throughout the provinces as he became more and more powerful.
5 The Jews killed all their enemies with the sword. They did as they pleased to those who hated them.
6 In the citadel of Susa, the Jews killed five hundred men,
7 including Parshandatha, Dalphon, Aspatha,
8 Poratha, Adalia, Aridatha,
9 Parmashta, Arisai, Aridai, and Vaizatha.
10 They killed these ten sons of Haman son of Hammedatha, the enemy of the Jews. But they did not lay a hand on the goods.
11 On that day the number killed in the citadel of Susa was reported to the king.

12 He said to Queen Esther, "In the citadel of Susa the Jews have killed five hundred men, including Haman's ten sons. What have they done in the rest of the king's provinces? Now what is your request? It will be given to you. And what more do you ask? It will be done."
13 Esther replied, "If it pleases the king, may the Jews in Susa also have tomorrow to carry out today's order. And may the bodies of Haman's ten sons be hanged on the pole."
14 So the king commanded that this be done. An order was issued in Susa, and they hanged the ten sons of Haman.
15 On the fourteenth day of the month of Adar, the Jews in Susa came together again. They killed three hundred men there, but they did not lay a hand on the goods.
16 The rest of the Jews in the king's provinces also gathered to defend themselves and free themselves from their enemies. They killed 75,000 who hated them, but they did not lay a hand on the goods.
17 This was on the thirteenth day of the month of Adar. On the fourteenth day they rested, making it a day of feasting and joy.
18 The Jews in Susa had gathered on the thirteenth and the fourteenth days. So they rested on the fifteenth day, making it a day of feasting and joy.
19 This is why the rural Jews who live in the villages observe the fourteenth day of Adar as a day of joy and feasting. It is a holiday for sending gifts to one another.
20 Mordecai recorded these events. He sent letters to all the Jews in all the king's provinces, both near and far.
21 He told them to celebrate the fourteenth and fifteenth days of Adar each year.

22 These were the days the Jews gained rest from their enemies. This was the month their sadness turned to joy and their grief into a feast day. He wrote that these were to be days of feasting and joy, of sending gifts to one another and to the poor.
23 So the Jews agreed to continue the custom they had started, as Mordecai had written.
24 For Haman son of Hammedatha, the Agagite, the enemy of all the Jews, had plotted against the Jews to destroy them. He had cast the Pur (that is, the lot) to crush and destroy them.
25 But when it came before the king, he commanded by letter that the evil plan Haman had made against the Jews should come back on his own head. And Haman and his sons should be hanged on the pole.
26 Therefore these days are called Purim, from the word Pur. Because of all the instructions in this letter, and because of all they had seen and experienced,
27 the Jews bound themselves to establish the custom. They and their descendants and all who join them must not fail to celebrate these two days at the right time each year, according to the rule.

28 These days should be remembered and celebrated by every generation, family, province, and city. The days of Purim should not be missed among the Jews. Their memory should not fade from their descendants.
29 So Queen Esther daughter of Abihail, with Mordecai the Jew, wrote with full power to confirm this second letter about Purim.
30 Mordecai sent letters with words of peace and truth to all the Jews in the 127 provinces of the kingdom of Xerxes,
31 to confirm these days of Purim at the right time, just as Mordecai the Jew and Queen Esther had set them. They had also committed themselves and their descendants to times of fasting and grieving.
32 So Esther's order confirmed these rules about Purim, which were written into the record.

title: Esther 10
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — Esther
paragraphs: 1 King Xerxes put a tax throughout the land, even to its farthest shores.
2 All of Mordecai's great and powerful works, together with the full story of the greatness to which the king had raised him, are written in the Book of the Records of the Kings of Media and Persia.
3 Mordecai the Jew was second only to King Xerxes. He was highly honored among the Jews and loved by his many people. He sought the good of his people and spoke peace to all his countrymen.
